title: Caching — Making Things Fast
subtitle: Browser cache, CDN, application cache, database cache, and Redis

sections.title: The Cache Layers
sections.text:
Caching means storing frequently used data somewhere fast so you don't have to recompute or re-fetch it. Caching happens everywhere:

  1. Browser Cache — Stores images, CSS, JS locally. Headers like Cache-Control: max-age=31536000 tell the browser "keep this for a year."
  2. CDN Cache — Content Delivery Networks (Cloudflare, Fastly) cache responses at servers close to the user. Your server handles far fewer requests.
  3. Application Cache — Your server caches database results in RAM using Redis or Memcached. Sub-millisecond responses instead of 50ms database queries.
  4. Database Cache — PostgreSQL and MySQL have internal caches too. Frequently accessed data stays in memory.

Every layer you add makes your app faster and your database happier.
sections.caption: Four layers of caching: browser → CDN → app → database
sections.code:
# Cache-Control headers
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable
Cache-Control: private, max-age=3600
Cache-Control: no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate

# ETag for conditional requests
ETag: "33a64df551425fcc55e4d42a148795d9f25f89d4"
If-None-Match: "33a64df5..."  → 304 Not Modified

sections.title: Cache Invalidation
sections.text:
There are only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things. How do you make sure users don't see stale data?

  • TTL (Time To Live) — Set a timer. "Cache this for 5 minutes." Simple, but users might see 5-minute-old data.
  • Cache-aside — Check cache first. On miss, query DB, update cache. Most common pattern.
  • Write-through — Update cache whenever DB changes. Always fresh, but more complex.
  • Event-driven — When data changes, publish an event that tells caches to update themselves.

The simplest approach that works: cache-aside with a reasonable TTL. Start there, get more sophisticated only when needed.

items: No cache → goes to origin server

sections.caption: Cache hit = fast response with no server load. Cache miss = full server processing.
sections.callout: The cache-aside pattern is the most common: check cache first, fall back to database on miss, update cache on read. Invalidate cache on writes.

sections.title: Redis — The Swiss Army Knife of Caching
sections.text:
Redis is an in-memory data store with sub-millisecond latency. It's the most popular tool for application caching, but it can do much more:

  • Cache — Store API responses, DB query results, rendered HTML. Everything has a TTL.
  • Sessions — Store user sessions across multiple server instances.
  • Rate Limiting — Track request counts per user/IP using INCR + EXPIRE.
  • Queues — Use lists (LPUSH/BRPOP) for background job processing.
  • Pub/Sub — Real-time messaging between services.
  • Real-time Leaderboards — Sorted sets with ZADD/ZRANGE for scores and rankings.

Redis is single-threaded for commands, which makes it predictable. Just don't run KEYS * in production — use SCAN.

items.heading: Hashes
items.body: Object-like: HSET user:1 name Alice. Perfect for cached DB rows.

items.heading: Lists
items.body: Ordered collections. LPUSH/RPUSH for queues, BRPOP for blocking reads.

items.heading: Sorted Sets
items.body: Ranked by score. ZADD/ZRANGE for leaderboards, rate limiting.

items.heading: Pub/Sub
items.body: Real-time messaging. PUBLISH channel message. SUBSCRIBE channel.

items.heading: TTL
items.body: EXPIRE key seconds. Keys auto-delete. Perfect for time-bounded caches.

sections.caption: Redis data structures — each designed for specific use cases
sections.code:
> SET user:1 '{"name":"Alice"}' EX 3600
OK
> GET user:1
"{\"name\":\"Alice\"}"
> INCR page:views:homepage
(integer) 42
> LPUSH queue:tasks 'send-email'
(integer) 1
> BRPOP queue:tasks 0
1) "queue:tasks"
2) "send-email"
